title.en: How to Read Bolt Markings: Identify Fastener Grade & Strength
title.zh: 如何阅读螺栓标记：识别紧固件等级与强度
title.es: Cómo leer las marcas de pernos: identificar grado y resistencia
title.ar: كيفية قراءة علامات البراغي: تحديد الدرجة والقوة
title.fr: Comment lire les marquages de boulons: identifier le grade et la résistance
title.pt: Como ler as marcações de parafusos: identificar grau e resistência

description.en: Complete guide to understanding bolt markings and head stamps. Learn how to identify bolt grades, tensile strength, and material properties from markings on bolt heads for correct fastener selection.
description.zh: 理解螺栓标记和头部印记的完整指南。学习如何从螺栓头部标记识别螺栓等级、拉伸强度和材料特性。
description.es: Guía completa para entender las marcas de pernos y sellos de cabeza.
description.ar: دليل شامل لفهم علامات البراغي وعلامات الرأس.
description.fr: Guide complet pour comprendre les marquages de boulons et les stamps de tête.
description.pt: Guia completo para entender as marcações de parafusos e selos de cabeça.

keywords: bolt markings, fastener grades, tensile strength, bolt identification, ISO 898, ASTM

body.en: Bolt markings provide critical information about the fastener's strength, material, and performance characteristics. Using the wrong grade bolt can lead to joint failure, equipment damage, and safety hazards. Understanding markings ensures you select the correct fastener for your application, preventing costly failures and safety incidents in African construction and industrial projects.

Metric bolts use a two-number grading system (e.g., 8.8, 10.9, 12.9). The first number represents 1/100 of the nominal tensile strength in N/mm². The second number is 10 times the ratio of proof stress to tensile strength. Higher numbers indicate stronger bolts.

Look for the grade marking on the bolt head. For metric bolts, the marking will show the two numbers separated by a decimal (e.g., 8.8). The marking may also include the manufacturer's trademark. For hex bolts, look for the marking on the top of the head. For socket head capscrews, the grade is typically marked on the side.

Stainless steel bolts use a different marking system. Look for A2 or A4 (property class) and the steel grade (e.g., 304, 316). A2 (304) is the most common stainless steel for general use. A4 (316) offers superior corrosion resistance for coastal and marine environments. Additional markings may indicate if the bolt is austenitic (A) or martensitic (C).

When sourcing fasteners for African projects, verify markings carefully: always confirm grade matches your specifications; be wary of counterfeit bolts with incorrect markings; for structural applications, insist on third-party test certificates; and store bolts by grade to prevent mixing. African construction projects have seen failures from counterfeit fasteners—always verify markings and source from reputable suppliers.
